result: I keep thinking about the way light looks on the inside of an empty coffee cup at 3:17 p.m., when the sun has slipped just far enough west to crawl across the kitchen table and illuminate the hairline cracks in the ceramic. Nothing important happens at 3:17; it is the dead minute between the end of lunch and the beginning of the long slope toward evening, when the refrigerator hum feels louder than thoughts. Yet that dull brown ring at the bottom of the cup suddenly glows like a map of some planet we haven’t bothered to name, and I feel invited—no, compelled—to stare until the planet spins and I’m dizzy with the certainty that every private despair I’ve ever rinsed down the sink is still orbiting, microscopic and glittering, in the municipal water. I imagine those griefs landing in other people’s kettles, steaming into their afternoon tea, seasoning their muffins with a taste they can’t quite identify. It’s ridiculous, of course; the water treatment plant murders everything, even memory. Still, I find myself washing the cup more gently, as if soap might be an act of violence against whatever small hospitable universe I’ve been hosting. Tomorrow I’ll drink from it again, add another layer of brown, another silent orbit. The cat will watch from the windowsill, tail twitching at invisible sparrows, and 3:17 will arrive whether or not I’m there to witness it, the light cracking open the day like a safe it already knows is empty.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
